title: Flag of Sweden

infobox.value: [Civil](/wiki/Civil_flag "Civil flag") and [state flag](/wiki/State_flag "State flag"), [civil](/wiki/Civil_ensign "Civil ensign") and [state ensign](/wiki/State_ensign "State ensign") [Small vexillological symbol or pictogram in black and white showing the different uses of the flag](/wiki/File:FIAV_110110.svg "Civil and state flags and ensigns") [Small vexillological symbol or pictogram in black and white showing the different uses of the flag](/wiki/File:FIAV_normal.svg "Normal or de jure version of flag, or obverse side") [Reverse side is mirror image of obverse side](/wiki/File:FIAV_mirror.svg "Reverse side is mirror image of obverse side")

A [blue](/wiki/Blue "Blue") field charged with a [yellow](/wiki/Yellow "Yellow") [Nordic cross](/wiki/Nordic_cross_flag "Nordic cross flag") that extends to the edges; the vertical part of the cross is shifted to the hoist side. Dimensions: 5:2:9 horizontally and 4:2:4 vertically.

The [national flag](/wiki/National_flag "National flag") of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") (*Sveriges flagga*) consists of a yellow or gold [Nordic cross](/wiki/Nordic_cross_flag "Nordic cross flag") (i.e. a horizontal cross extending to the edges, with the crossbar closer to the hoist than the fly) on a field of light blue. The Nordic cross design traditionally represents [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").[[2]](#cite_note-Jeroen_Temperman-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Carol_A._Foley-3) The design and colours of the Swedish flag are believed to have been inspired by the present [coat of arms of Sweden](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Sweden "Coat of arms of Sweden") of 1442, which is blue divided quarterly by a [cross pattée](/wiki/Cross_patt%C3%A9e "Cross pattée") of gold. Blue and yellow have been used as Swedish colours at least since [Magnus III](/wiki/Magnus_III_of_Sweden "Magnus III of Sweden")'s royal coat of arms of 1275.

## Specifics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Specifics")]

### Ratio and colour scheme

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ratio and colour scheme")]

The Swedish flag is one of only five that use the ratio 5:8, the others being the flags of [Argentina](/wiki/Flag_of_Argentina "Flag of Argentina"), [Guatemala](/wiki/Flag_of_Guatemala "Flag of Guatemala"), [Palau](/wiki/Flag_of_Palau "Flag of Palau"), and [Poland](/wiki/Flag_of_Poland "Flag of Poland").

It is one of only five flags that currently use the colour scheme of [blue](/wiki/Blue "Blue") and [yellow](/wiki/Yellow "Yellow"), the others being the flags of [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Flag_of_Kazakhstan "Flag of Kazakhstan"), [Palau](/wiki/Flag_of_Palau "Flag of Palau"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Flag_of_Ukraine "Flag of Ukraine"), and the [European Union](/wiki/Flag_of_Europe "Flag of Europe").

### State flag and civil ensign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: State flag and civil ensign")]

The dimensions of the Swedish flag are 5:2:9 horizontally and 4:2:4 vertically. The dimensions of the Swedish flag with a triple-tail are 5:2:5:8 horizontally and 4:2:4 vertically.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The colours of the flag are officially established through the [Natural Color System](/wiki/Natural_Color_System "Natural Color System") to be [NCS](/wiki/Natural_Color_System "Natural Color System") 0580-Y10R for the shade of yellow, and NCS 4055-R95B for the shade of blue.[[5]](#cite_note-5) They are also specified to [Pantone](/wiki/Pantone "Pantone") *PMS 301 C/U* for blue and *PMS 116* or *PMS 109 U* for yellow.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The square-cut Swedish state flag is identical to the civil ensign. The Swedish law does not regulate the design of the Swedish [pennant](/wiki/Pennon "Pennon"), but it is recommended that its colour scheme should correspond with that of the flag.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Naval ensign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Naval ensign")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/HMS_Gustav_V.jpg/250px-HMS_Gustav_V.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Gustav_V.jpg)

Coastal defence ship [HSwMS *Gustaf V*](/wiki/HSwMS_Gustaf_V "HSwMS Gustaf V") in 1957, with the current naval ensign, and the greater coat of arms in the stern (Agfacolor).

The triple-tailed flag (*tretungad flagga*, lit. 'three-tongued flag') is used as a [naval ensign](/wiki/Naval_ensign "Naval ensign") (*örlogsflaggan*). Its overall ratio, including the tails, is 1:2. The flag is also used as the Swedish [naval jack](/wiki/Naval_jack "Naval jack") (*örlogsgösen*). The jacks are smaller than the ensigns, but they have the same proportions. The Swedish swallowtail flag was originally the King's personal emblem, or the emblem representing a command conferred by the King. It was at first two-pointed, but by the mid-17th century, the distinctive swallowtail with tongue appeared. The flag is also flown by the defence ministry, while civil ministries fly square flags.

### Royal flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Royal flag")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Ulriksdal_Kungaflaggen_2011a.jpg/250px-Ulriksdal_Kungaflaggen_2011a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ulriksdal_Kungaflaggen_2011a.jpg)

Royal Flag of Sweden flying over the [Ulriksdal Palace](/wiki/Ulriksdal_Palace "Ulriksdal Palace")

The royal flag (*kungliga flaggan*) is identical to the triple-tailed naval ensign, but has in its centre a white field with the [greater or the lesser coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Sweden "Coat of arms of Sweden") surrounded by the [Order of the Seraphim](/wiki/Royal_Order_of_the_Seraphim "Royal Order of the Seraphim"), which has the [monarch](/wiki/Monarch_of_Sweden "Monarch of Sweden") as its grand master. The monarch personally decides about the specific use of the royal flag.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

* [![Royal standard of Sweden with the greater coat of arms, used by the king and queen](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Royal_standard_of_Sweden.svg/500px-Royal_standard_of_Sweden.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_standard_of_Sweden.svg "Royal standard of Sweden with the greater coat of arms, used by the king and queen")

* [![Royal standard of Sweden with the lesser coat of arms, used by other members of the Royal House.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Sweden-Royal-flag-lesser-coa.svg/500px-Sweden-Royal-flag-lesser-coa.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Sweden-Royal-flag-lesser-coa.svg "Royal standard of Sweden with the lesser coat of arms, used by other members of the Royal House.")

Generally, the king and queen use a royal flag with the greater national coat of arms. Other members of the royal house use a royal flag with the lesser national coat of arms. On naval ships the flag of the king is raised together with a split pennant with the greater national coat of arms. Likewise the flag of the heir apparent is raised together with a split pennant with the lesser national coat of arms on naval ships.

The [Swedish Marshal of the Realm](/wiki/Marshal_of_the_Realm_(Sweden) "Marshal of the Realm (Sweden)") (*Riksmarskalken*) has published a series of decisions regarding the royal flag of Sweden. In a decision of 6 April 1987, rules are defined on how to fly the royal flag at the Royal Palace of Stockholm.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

* The royal flag with the greater national coat of arms is hoisted at the Royal Palace when H.M. the King is within the realm, and is upholding his duties as the Head of state.
* The royal flag with the lesser national coat of arms is hoisted at the Royal Palace, if by reason of illness, foreign travel or for any other cause, the King is unavoidably prevented from performing his duties; a member of the Royal House under the valid order of succession who is not prevented therefrom, assumes and performs the duties of the Head of State in the capacity of Regent ad interim.
* The "plain" triple-tailed flag (without the coat of arms) is flown at the Royal Palace when the [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag") has appointed a person to serve, at a Government order, as Regent *ad interim* when no member of the Royal House under the valid order of succession is in a position to serve. The three-tailed war flag is also flown at the Royal Palace when the Speaker, or, in his unavoidable absence, one of the Deputy Speakers, serves, at a Government order, as Regent *ad interim* when no member of the Royal House under the valid order of succession is in a position to serve.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Under [King Carl XVI Gustaf](/wiki/Carl_XVI_Gustaf_of_Sweden "Carl XVI Gustaf of Sweden"), king since 1973, the plain triple-tailed flag has flown at the Royal Palace only once. This occurred on 2–3 July 1988, when the King went on a private visit to [Wuppertal](/wiki/Wuppertal "Wuppertal"), West Germany; at the same time, the [Duke of Halland](/wiki/Prince_Bertil,_Duke_of_Halland "Prince Bertil, Duke of Halland"), his uncle and the only person in the [line of succession](/wiki/Line_of_succession_to_the_Swedish_throne "Line of succession to the Swedish throne"), was on a private visit to [Sainte-Maxime](/wiki/Sainte-Maxime "Sainte-Maxime"), France. The government therefore ordered the [speaker of the Riksdag](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_Riksdag "Speaker of the Riksdag"), [Ingemund Bengtsson](/wiki/Ingemund_Bengtsson "Ingemund Bengtsson"), to serve as regent *ad interim* for two days.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History")]

### Royal banners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Royal banners")]

Prior to the adoption of the national Swedish flag featuring the [Nordic cross](/wiki/Nordic_cross "Nordic cross"), there were Royal banners, often featuring a striped field, often featuring blue, white, and yellow. Blue and yellow as national colors dates back to the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), and have been featured on the National emblem of Sweden, the [Three Crowns](/wiki/Three_Crowns "Three Crowns"), since at least 1336.[[14]](#cite_note-Heraldisk_Tidsskrift-14)

### Nordic cross flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Nordic cross flag")]

Further information: [Nordic cross flag](/wiki/Nordic_cross_flag "Nordic cross flag")

The exact age of the Swedish flag featuring the Nordic cross is not known. Other cross flags, such as the [Flag of Denmark](/wiki/Flag_of_Denmark "Flag of Denmark"), [Flag of England](/wiki/Flag_of_England "Flag of England"), [Flag of Constantinople](/wiki/Byzantine_flags_and_insignia "Byzantine flags and insignia"), etc, started appearing already in the 13th century, and most likely influenced the Swedish flag design.

According to early modern legend and popular folklore, the 12th-century [King Eric IX](/wiki/Eric_IX_of_Sweden "Eric IX of Sweden") saw the sun shine against a golden cross in the sky as he landed in Finland during the [First Swedish Crusade](/wiki/First_Swedish_Crusade "First Swedish Crusade") in 1157. Seeing this as a sign from God he adopted the golden cross against a blue background as his banner.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The oldest recorded pictures of a blue cloth with a yellow cross date from the early 16th century, during the reign of [King Gustav I](/wiki/Gustav_I_of_Sweden "Gustav I of Sweden").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The first legal description of the flag was made in a royal warrant of 19 April 1562 as: "yellow in a cross fashioned on blue" (*gult udi korssvijs fördeelt påå blott*).[[15]](#cite_note-15) As stipulated in a royal warrant of 1569, the yellow cross was always to be borne on Swedish battle standards and banners. Prior to this, a similar flag appeared in the coat of arms of [King John III](/wiki/John_III_of_Sweden "John III of Sweden")'s duchy, which is today [Southwest Finland](/wiki/Southwest_Finland "Southwest Finland"). The same coat of arms is still used by the province.

A potential predecessor to the Swedish flag with the Nordic cross could be the [coat of arms of Sweden](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Sweden "Coat of arms of Sweden"), introduced during the reign of [King Charles VIII](/wiki/Karl_Knutsson "Karl Knutsson") in 1448. The national coat of arms is a combination of [King Albert](/wiki/Albert_of_Sweden "Albert of Sweden")'s coat of arms of 1364 and [King Magnus III](/wiki/Magnus_III_of_Sweden "Magnus III of Sweden")'s coat of arms of 1275, and is blue divided quarterly by a golden [cross pattée](/wiki/Cross_patt%C3%A9e "Cross pattée"). Other ideas claim that the Swedish flag was blue with a white cross before 1420, and became blue with a golden cross a century later during the early reign of [King Gustav I](/wiki/Gustav_I_of_Sweden "Gustav I of Sweden").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Triple-tailed flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Triple-tailed flag")]

A royal warrant of 6 November 1663, regulated the use of the triple-tailed flag, to be used only as a state flag and naval ensign.[[1]](#cite_note-plakat-1) According to the same royal warrant, merchant ships were only allowed to fly square-cut city flags in their respective provincial colours. In practice, however, the merchant fleet began using a square-cut civil ensign of the state flag. In a government instruction of ship building of 1730, this civil ensign should have the same proportions and colors as the state flag, with the notable difference of being square-cut.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1756, the use of pennants by private ships was prohibited.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

#### Blue ensign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Blue ensign")]

A royal warrant of 18 August 1761, stipulated that an all blue triple-tailed flag to be used by the [archipelago fleet](/wiki/Archipelago_fleet "Archipelago fleet"), a branch of the [army](/wiki/Swedish_army "Swedish army") tasked with defending the archipelago along the Swedish coastlines. The commander of the fleet also had the right to order the use of the ordinary war ensign instead of the blue ensign when that was "appropriate".[[18]](#cite_note-18) The blue flag was used until 1813.

### Union between Sweden and Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Union between Sweden and Norway")]

#### Union flags of 1815 and 1818

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Union flags of 1815 and 1818")]

On 6 June 1815, a common military ensign was introduced for the two united kingdoms of Sweden and Norway. This flag was identical to the former triple-tailed military ensign of Sweden, with a white [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire") on red to be included in the [canton](/wiki/Canton_(heraldry) "Canton (heraldry)"). Proposed by the Norwegian [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Norway "Prime Minister of Norway") and unionist [Peder Anker](/wiki/Peder_Anker "Peder Anker"), the white saltire on a red background was supposed to symbolise Norway, as the country had previously been united with Denmark and initially continued to use the same flag as an independent country, but with the national arms in the canton.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Norwegian ships continued to use the Danish civil ensign distinguished with the national arms in the canton north of [Cape Finisterre](/wiki/Cape_Finisterre "Cape Finisterre"), but had to fly the Swedish civil ensign in the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") to be protected from pirate attacks. A common civil ensign for both countries was introduced in 1818, on the pattern of the naval ensign, but square-cut. This flag was optional for Swedish vessels, but compulsory for Norwegian ones in distant waters. In 1821, Norway adopted a new national civil ensign, identical to the present [flag of Norway](/wiki/Flag_of_Norway "Flag of Norway").

  Swedish [sloop](/wiki/Sloop "Sloop") with the early Union flag and pennant (1822)

Following the adoption of a separate Norwegian flag, a royal regulation of 17 July 1821, stipulated that ships of both kingdoms use the common square-cut civil ensign (with the saltire included) in "distant waters" (*i.e.* beyond [Cape Finisterre](/wiki/Cape_Finisterre "Cape Finisterre")).[[20]](#cite_note-20) In "distant waters", they had the right to use any of the square-cut civil ensigns of their respective countries, or the uniform Union civil ensign. This system was in force until 1838.

#### Union flags of 1844

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Union flags of 1844")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Flaggsirkul%C3%A6re_1899.jpg/250px-Flaggsirkul%C3%A6re_1899.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flaggsirkul%C3%A6re_1899.jpg)

Flags of Sweden and Norway in 1899, after the removal of the union mark from the Norwegian merchant flag. Plate published by the Swedish-Norwegian foreign ministry to announce the recent change.

A royal resolution of 20 June 1844, introduced new flags and heraldry to denote the equal status of the two kingdoms within the union. Both countries were granted civil and military ensigns on the same pattern, their respective national flags with the addition of a [union mark](/wiki/Union_mark_of_Norway_and_Sweden "Union mark of Norway and Sweden") in the canton, combining the flag colours of both countries. The naval ensign was based on the traditional triple-tailed Swedish model.

In addition, the new union mark was to be used as the naval jack and as the flag for the common diplomatic representations abroad. The warrant also stipulated that the merchant fleet use their respective countries' square-cut civil ensigns, including the new union mark. Also, royal ensigns were introduced for both countries, their respective naval ensigns with the union mark, with the addition of the union arms at the center of the cross.

The new union flags were well received by the Norwegians, who had demanded their own military ensign since the union was formed. In Sweden, however, the new union mark in particular became quite unpopular and was contemptuously nicknamed the *Sillsallaten* (Swedish) or *Sildesalaten* (Norwegian) after a colorful dish of pickled herring, decorated with red beets and apples in a radial pattern. It is believed that the name was first used in a speech by Lord Brakel in the [Swedish House of Lords](/wiki/Swedish_House_of_Lords "Swedish House of Lords") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

During the 19th century, a number of regulations were issued regarding the use of Swedish flags. The military ensign was also to be used by civil government ships and buildings, such as the [Customs](/wiki/Customs "Customs"), [Harbor pilots](/wiki/Harbor_pilot "Harbor pilot") and the [Royal Mail](/wiki/Posten_(Sweden) "Posten (Sweden)"). For this use, the military ensign would have a white field included with a golden marker: For the Harbor pilots (as of 1881, based on a proposal of 1825) an anchor with a star;[[22]](#cite_note-23) for the Customs (as of 1844) the letter "T" topped a royal crown;[[23]](#cite_note-24) for the Royal Mail (as of 1844) a postal horn with a royal crown. On 7 May 1897, an alternate state flag was introduced. This double-tailed flag was used by government owned ships and buildings, which did not fly the triple-tailed military ensign.

During the late 19th century, increasing Norwegian dissatisfaction with the union led to the demand for a return to the "pure" flag of 1821 without the union mark. Opponents of the union began to use this flag several years before it was officially recognised. During the 1890s, two consecutive sessions of the Norwegian parliament voted to abolish the mark, but the decision was overruled by royal veto. However, in 1898, when the flag law was passed for the third time, the king had to sanction it. On 12 October 1899, the union mark was removed from the Norwegian civil ensign. As the Norwegian military ensign according to the constitution of 1814 was to be a union ensign, the union mark remained on military flags until the dissolution of the union with Sweden. "Pure" military ensigns were hoisted on fortresses and naval vessels on 9 June 1905.

The union mark, however, remained a part of the Swedish flag until 1905, when a Law of 28 October 1905, stipulated the removal of the union mark as of 1 November 1905.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

### Colour change in 1906

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Colour change in 1906")]

Following the [dissolution of the union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_union_between_Norway_and_Sweden "Dissolution of the union between Norway and Sweden") in 1905, the triple-tailed naval ensign also became the Swedish naval jack, replacing the common naval jack, and the Flag law of 22 June 1906 further regulated the use and design of the flag, specifying the colours to be "ljust mellanblå" (light medium blue) and "guldgul" (golden yellow), a departure from the previously darker shade of blue. The Swedish state flag became identical to the square-cut civil ensign, and all private use of the triple-tailed ensign was prohibited.

### Private use of the state flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Private use of the state flag")]

There have been a few notable exemptions regarding the prohibition of private parties to use the state flag. All these privileges were terminated in accordance with the new specific flag regulation of 22 June 1906.

* According to a royal warrant of 31 October 1786, the [Swedish East India Company](/wiki/Swedish_East_India_Company "Swedish East India Company") had the right to use the triple-tailed war ensign in "Indian waters", when *not* being under immediate protection by the [Swedish navy](/wiki/Swedish_Navy "Swedish Navy"). In the merchant fleet, there was a common practice to illegally use the war ensign to indicate that the ship was armed.[[25]](#cite_note-26)
* In 1838, it was decided that private ships contracted by the Royal Mail were to fly a double-tailed flag.
* On 27 February 1832, the [Royal Swedish Yacht Club](/wiki/Royal_Swedish_Yacht_Club "Royal Swedish Yacht Club") received the right to use the triple-tailed war ensign, including a centre white field with a golden "O" topped with a duke's crown (as of 1878 a royal crown).
* On 7 June 1893, the [Royal Gothenburg Yacht Club](/wiki/Royal_Gothenburg_Yacht_Club "Royal Gothenburg Yacht Club") received the right to use the triple-tailed military ensign, including a centered white field with the golden "G K S S" topped with a star.

## Use outside Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Use outside Sweden")]

The flag of Sweden appears in coat of arms of [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")'s province [Southwest Finland](/wiki/Southwest_Finland "Southwest Finland").

The flag of [Wilmington, Delaware](/wiki/Wilmington,_Delaware "Wilmington, Delaware") in the United States is modelled after the Swedish flag in remembrance of the short-lived colony of [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden"), with the cross affixed with the seal of the city. The [flag of Philadelphia](/wiki/Flag_of_Philadelphia "Flag of Philadelphia"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), which also lies within historic New Sweden, is a vertical [triband](/wiki/Triband_(flag) "Triband (flag)") rather than a Nordic Cross, but the blue and yellow colours of the flag were chosen to commemorate the Swedish settlement of the region.[[26]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-27) According to a legend, the Argentine [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") team [Boca Juniors](/wiki/Boca_Juniors "Boca Juniors")' flag and colours were inspired by the flag of Sweden.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The seal of the Municipal Council of [Shanghai International Settlement](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Settlement "Shanghai International Settlement") incorporated the 1844 Swedish civil ensign.

## Rules for displaying the Swedish flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rules for displaying the Swedish flag")]

When used from a stand-alone flagpole, the size of the flag is recommended to have a width equalling a fourth of the height of the pole. When used from a flagpole extending from a building, the flag is recommended to have a width equalling a third of the height (length) of the pole.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

The times and rules for raising and lowering the Swedish flag are as follows:[[29]](#cite_note-30)

* Between 1 March and 31 October the flag can be raised from 08:00.
* Between 1 November and 28/29 February the flag can be raised from 09:00.
* The flag shall be taken down at sunset but at the latest 21:00.
* If the flag is illuminated, it may remain up even after the sun has set.

If the flag is no longer in presentable condition it should be discarded by burning in a respectful manner or returned to the manufacturer for disposal.

Private citizens are not obliged to fly the flag on official [flag flying days](/wiki/Flag_flying_day "Flag flying day"), but they are encouraged to do it. Apart from the [flag flying days in Sweden](/wiki/Flag_flying_days_in_Sweden "Flag flying days in Sweden"), everyone is of course able to hoist the flag whenever there is a reason for celebration in the family or otherwise.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

## Pennant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Pennant")]

Main article: [Household pennant](/wiki/Household_pennant "Household pennant")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Pend%C3%B3n_sueco_en_Visby.jpg/330px-Pend%C3%B3n_sueco_en_Visby.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pend%C3%B3n_sueco_en_Visby.jpg)

Swedish pennant flying

The Swedish pennant is shaped like an isosceles triangle (to prevent fraying, it is sometimes shaped like an isosceles trapezoid), consisting of two horizontal bands with blue at the top and yellow at the bottom. The National Archives of Sweden recommends its length to be 1⁄3 the height of the flag pole. There is also an unofficial version of the pennant which resembles the national flag, with a yellow cross on a blue field, which is advised not to be used.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33)
